title: Les verbes être et avoir
description: The two most important French verbs. Present-tense conjugation + when to use each.

cards.notes: Used with friends, family, kids, peers. NEVER for a stranger or a customer — that's *vous*.
Also used for masculine objects: *le livre — il est rouge* (the book — it is red).
Also used for feminine objects: *la chaise — elle est verte*.
In casual speech, often replaced by *on* (*on est* = we are). At A1, stick with *nous*.
Two uses: (1) formal singular (one stranger, one professor), (2) plural (multiple people, any formality). The verb form is the same.
Used for groups of all men or mixed men+women. Even one man in a group of women makes the group *ils*.
Only when every member of the group is feminine.
Note the circumflex on the ê — it's a hint that an old s used to be there (compare English *est*).
No accent. *il a* (he has) vs *il à* (which doesn't exist as a sentence — *à* is a preposition).
Don't confuse with *sont* (they are). One letter, totally different meaning.

steps.title: The two most important verbs
steps.body:
# *Être* and *avoir*

If you only learn two French verbs at A1, learn **être** (to be) and **avoir** (to have). Together they cover:

- Identity: *Je **suis** étudiant.* — I am a student.
- Description: *Elle **est** grande.* — She is tall.
- Possession: *J'**ai** un chat.* — I have a cat.
- Age: *Tu **as** vingt ans.* — You are twenty (lit.: you have twenty years).
- Plus the **passé composé** — French's main past tense — is built from *avoir* + past participle (Lesson 10).

Both verbs are **irregular** — the forms don't follow a pattern, you have to memorise them. Same in English (am / are / is / was / were), same in Spanish (soy / eres / es), same in German (bin / bist / ist). It's worth the upfront effort.

steps.title: Être — to be
steps.body:
## Present tense of *être*

| Subject | Form |
|---|---|
| je | **suis** |
| tu | **es** |
| il / elle | **est** |
| nous | **sommes** |
| vous | **êtes** |
| ils / elles | **sont** |

Pronunciation traps:
- *suis* — silent **s** at the end, sounds like "swee"
- *est* — the **s** and **t** are silent: just "eh"
- *êtes* — the circumflex marks an old **s**: pronounced "ett"
- *sont* — nasal vowel + silent **t**: "sohn"

Use *être* for: identity, profession, nationality, description, location.

*Je **suis** français. Tu **es** ici. Nous **sommes** prêts.*

exercise.prompt: Match each subject pronoun with the correct form of *être*.
Type the form of *être* that goes with **vous**.

steps.title: Avoir — to have
steps.body:
## Present tense of *avoir*

| Subject | Form |
|---|---|
| je (j') | **ai** |
| tu | **as** |
| il / elle | **a** |
| nous | **avons** |
| vous | **avez** |
| ils / elles | **ont** |

Mandatory elision: *je* + *ai* always becomes **j'ai**. You'll never see *je ai*.

Use *avoir* for: possession, age, certain idioms.

- *J'**ai** trois enfants.* — I have three children.
- *Vous **avez** vingt ans.* — You are twenty years old. (Literally: you HAVE twenty years.)
- *Ils **ont** faim.* — They are hungry. (Literally: they HAVE hunger.)

French uses *avoir* where English uses *be* for age, hunger, thirst, fear, and being right or wrong. Translate by meaning, not word-for-word.

exercise.prompt: Match each subject pronoun with the correct form of *avoir*.
Type the form of *avoir* that goes with **nous**.

steps.title: Tu or vous?
steps.body:
## The *tu* / *vous* choice

French requires you to pick one of two forms for "you":

| Situation | Use |
|---|---|
| Talking to one friend, family member, child | **tu** |
| Talking to a stranger, customer, professor (one person) | **vous** |
| Talking to more than one person, any formality | **vous** |

Note that **vous** does double duty (formal singular AND any plural). The verb conjugation is the same — context tells you which one is meant.

When in doubt with someone new, use **vous**. They'll let you know if it's OK to switch to **tu** ("*on se tutoie*" — let's use tu).

At A1, when you don't know which to pick, **vous** is the safer choice.

exercise.prompt: You are addressing your professor in class. Which pronoun do you use?
exercise.hint: Professors get the formal form, even if they're young.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the tiles to mean *I am French* (using masculine form).
exercise.hint: Subject + être + adjective. Same order as English.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the tiles to mean *I am hungry*. (Hint: French uses *avoir*, not *être*, for hunger.)
exercise.hint: Literally *I have hunger* — French uses avoir for hunger / thirst / age.

exercise.prompt: Translate *I am 25 years old.* into French. (Hint: French uses *avoir* for age. Write out the number as a single word: *vingt-cinq*.)
exercise.hint: Start with *J'ai* (I have), then the number, then *ans* (years).

exercise.prompt: Match each English phrase to the French verb it uses.
Fill in the right form of *avoir* or *être*.
exercise.hint: *Elle a une amie* = *She has a friend* — possession uses *avoir*. *Elle est une amie* would mean *She IS a friend* (identity, using *être*), which is grammatically valid but a different sentence.
